Abstract: Large language model (LLM) safety classifiers such as Llama Guard are effective at detecting overtly harmful prompts but remain vulnerable to adversarial jailbreak attacks that disguise malicious intent through role-play scenarios, fictional framing, and indirect requests. We present Reflect-Guard, a method that augments LLM-based safety classifiers with chain-of-thought self-reflection capabilities through parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Our approach distills analytical reasoning from GPT-4o-mini into structured reflection annotations, then trains Llama-Guard-3-8B via QLoRA to generate logical self-reflections before issuing safety verdicts. Using only 1000 training examples and updating just 0.5% of model parameters (~42M), Reflect-Guard achieves substantial improvements on two challenging benchmarks. On WildGuardTest, F1 score improves from 0.770 to 0.842 (+7.2 pp), with recall on adversarial prompts increasing from 0.513 to 0.921 (+40.8 pp). On JailbreakBench, the attack success rate drops from 10.3% to 1.8%, representing an 82.5% relative reduction. These gains are especially pronounced on adversarial inputs, where the explicit reasoning step enables the model to see through obfuscation techniques that defeat standard pattern-matching approaches. Our results demonstrate that teaching safety classifiers to reason about adversarial intent, rather than simply classify surface patterns, is a promising direction for robust LLM safety.

</details>


### [448] [Pre-Characterization of Electromagnetic Side-Channel Leakage Using Publicly Available Information: A Case Study on E-Voting Interfaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25142)
*Leonardo Teodoro,Kemuel L. Vieira,Saulo Queiroz*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper investigates the susceptibility of the Brazilian e-Voting Machine to TEMPEST attacks, showing that its interface produces a distinct electromagnetic signature that can be exploited for eavesdropping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to assess the security vulnerabilities of the Brazilian e-Voting Machine against electromagnetic side-channel attacks, specifically TEMPEST attacks.

Method: The authors emulate the Brazilian e-Voting Machine using a VGA monitor and publicly available specifications from electoral authorities, then analyze the electromagnetic emissions during video signal transmission.

Result: The experiments indicate that the BVM interface emits a distinctive spectral signature, influenced by design features like high contrast and minimal information display, which can be intercepted at a distance.

Conclusion: The findings highlight potential vulnerabilities of the BVM to TEMPEST attacks and suggest the need for protective measures, including the design of adaptive jammers.

Abstract: In this work, we study the interface of the Brazilian e-Voting Machine (BVM) in the context of electromagnetic side-channel threats commonly referred to as TEMPEST attacks. In a TEMPEST attack against video displays, an eavesdropper uses Software-Defined Radios (SDRs) to recover sensitive information by intercepting electromagnetic emanations generated during video signal transmission. We emulate the BVM using a VGA monitor by leveraging publicly available information disclosed by the electoral authority, including technical specifications, operational rules of the system, and the official BVM interface. Based on this setup, we investigate whether the BVM interface gives rise to a distinctive spectral signature observable through its unintended electromagnetic emissions. Our findings show that design characteristics relevant to a nationwide electoral process -- such as high image contrast, minimal on-screen information, and the prohibition of other electronic devices within the polling station -- result in a simple and highly distinctive spectral signature that can be observed even through a wall in our experiments. Although our experiments do not involve actual BVM hardware, the results raise concerns regarding the system's susceptibility to TEMPEST attacks and highlight the need for further research on protective countermeasures. In this context, our findings may support the design of automatic jammers capable of adaptively targeting compromising frequencies. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study investigating TEMPEST attacks in the context of an electronic voting system officially adopted by a country.

</details>


### [449] [SEED: Semi-supervised Continual MalwarE Detection for Tackling ConcEpt Drift on a BuDget](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24903)
*Suresh Kumar Amalapuram,Bikraj Shresta,Siva Ram murthy Chebiyam,Bheemarjuna Reddy Tamma,Sumohana S Channappayya*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper presents SEED, a new method for malware detection that operates effectively under limited supervision, addressing the challenges of concept drift and the scarcity of labeled data in the security domain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current malware detection methods suffer from performance degradation due to concept drift and the challenge of obtaining sufficient labeled data, particularly in security applications.

Method: SEED utilizes a customized binary cross-entropy objective combined with semi-supervised continual learning and active learning. It projects unlabeled samples into a representation space using singular value decomposition and selects uncertain samples for labeling based on cosine distance for unseen tasks.

Result: SEED demonstrates significant improvements in unseen malware detection, achieving 40% AUT improvement on BODMAS and 14% on AndroZoo with only 20% labeled data for seen tasks, while showing competitive performance on APIGraph.

Conclusion: The introduction of a delayed buffer update strategy enhances learning stability and minimizes label noise propagation in malware detection.

Abstract: Machine learning based malware detectors become obsolete over time due to concept drift in benign and malware applications. Recent methods rely on fully labeled data and use hierarchical contrastive loss (HCL) with active learning to improve robustness against drift by exploiting semantic structure in malware representations. However, obtaining labeled data in the security domain is difficult. Under partially labeled settings, HCL suffers significant performance degradation in detecting unseen malware, especially on datasets such as BODMAS where strong semantic structure may not exist. In this paper, we propose SEED, a semantic-structure-agnostic method for malware detection under limited supervision. SEED combines a tailored binary cross-entropy objective with semi-supervised continual learning and active learning. For partially labeled seen tasks, unlabeled samples are projected into a representation space constructed from previously seen data using singular value decomposition, and paired with suitable labeled samples to encourage representation consistency. For unseen tasks with fully unlabeled data, uncertainty is quantified using cosine distance in representation space, and the most uncertain samples are selected for analyst labeling. We evaluate SEED on both Windows and Android malware datasets. Using only 20% labeled data on seen tasks, SEED achieves average AUT improvements of 40% on BODMAS and 14% on AndroZoo for unseen malware detection compared to HCL* (the semi-supervised adaptation of HCL), while remaining competitive on APIGraph. Finally, we introduce a delayed buffer update strategy to reduce label noise propagation during replay and improve learning stability.

</details>


### [450] [Memory-Induced Tool-Drift in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24941)
*Mahavir Dabas,Jihyun Jeong,Ming Jin,Ruoxi Jia*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper investigates memory-induced tool-drift in LLM agents, where biases in long-term memory unintentionally affect tool calls inappropriately. It introduces the MEMDRIFT benchmark to quantify this phenomenon, revealing significant deviations in performance across various models and tools, and highlights the need for improved defenses against these vulnerabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how personality-driven biases in memory can impact decision-making in tool-calling LLM agents, which is critical for ensuring reliable tool interactions in real-world applications.

Method: The study operationalizes memory-induced tool-drift through MEMDRIFT, a benchmark comprising 105 scenarios assessing biases across five dimensions in seven professional domains, and evaluates performance using deflection scores in various LLM models and real-world tools.

Result: Up to a +3.6 increase in deflection scores is observed across multiple models due to biased memories, which also affects a significant number of tools identified as having susceptible parameters.

Conclusion: Memory-induced tool-drift represents a systematic vulnerability in LLM agents, necessitating enhanced memory management strategies that specifically target the interplay between biases and tool interaction, as current defenses are insufficient.

Abstract: Modern LLM agents combine long-term memory for personalization with tool-calling interfaces for taking actions in the world -- a combination underpinning contemporary production systems. We study a previously unexamined failure of this combination: when personality-driven biases stored in memory (cost-consciousness, impatience, risk tolerance, etc.) silently affect tool calls in contexts where they are not applicable. We call this memory-induced tool-drift and operationalize it through MEMDRIFT, a benchmark of 105 scenarios spanning five bias dimensions and seven professional domains, generated through an automated adversarial pipeline. Across seven frontier models -- including those with extended reasoning -- biased memories raise deflection scores (a judge-scored measure of parameter deviation from unbiased baselines) by up to $+3.6$ points on a 1--5 scale. Tool-drift persists when memory management is handled by three production memory architectures. The phenomenon affects real-world tools: scanning 6{,}062 tools across 288 verified MCP servers, we flag 608 with susceptible parameters and confirm tool-drift on a validated subset. Mechanistically, biased memories act as implicit steering vectors, pushing activations along the same latent directions as explicit behavioral instructions. They also redistribute attention from task-relevant context toward memory entries with surface-level keyword overlap to the target parameter. Standard defenses -- prompt-based relevance instructions and memory filters -- reduce drift but do not eliminate it. As agents take increasingly consequential actions on a user's behalf, memory-induced tool-drift represents a systematic vulnerability that current safeguards do not address, motivating dedicated defenses at the intersection of memory management and tool-call generation.

</details>


### [451] [APT-Agent: Automated Penetration Testing using Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24949)
*William Guanting Li,Alsharif Abuadbba,Kristen Moore,Dan Dongseong Kim*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: APT-Agent is a novel LLM-driven framework for automated penetration testing that addresses challenges of hallucinations and context retention, achieving significantly higher exploitation success rates than existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The increasing scale and complexity of web infrastructures require more efficient penetration testing methods than traditional manual approaches can provide.

Method: APT-Agent employs a hybrid rectification module to fix hallucinated commands and a command-specific memory structure to maintain context during multi-step attacks, thus automating reconnaissance, exploitation, and exfiltration.

Result: APT-Agent demonstrates an end-to-end exploitation success rate of 84.29% on Metasploitable 2, outperforming Script Kiddie (48.57%) and PentestGPT (18.57%) under similar testing conditions.

Conclusion: APT-Agent enhances the automation of penetration testing by reducing cognitive load and reliance on human operators, making it a more scalable and effective solution.

Abstract: Penetration testing is essential to securing modern web infrastructures, yet traditional manual methods struggle to keep pace with their scale and complexity. Large Language Models (LLMs) offer new opportunities for automating these tasks, but existing approaches face two persistent challenges: hallucination of technical entities and insufficient long-term contextual memory. To address these issues, we present APT-Agent, a fully automated LLM-driven penetration testing framework that systematically orchestrates reconnaissance, exploitation, and exfiltration. APT-Agent introduces a hybrid rectification module to recover hallucinated commands and a command-specific memory architecture to preserve operational context across multi-step attack sequences. We evaluate our APT-Agent on Metasploitable 2 against seven vulnerable services spanning web, database, and network protocols. APT-Agent achieves an 84.29% end-to-end exploitation success rate, compared to 48.57% (Script Kiddie) and 18.57% (PentestGPT) under matched conditions. By reducing cognitive burden and minimizing reliance on human intervention, APT-Agent represents a step toward scalable, reliable, and cognitively efficient automation for penetration testing.

</details>


### [452] [An Efficient and Privacy-Preserving Architecture for Cross-Institutional Collaborative RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25716)
*Chenxin Mao,Shangyu Liu,Zhenzhe Zheng,Fan Wu,Jie Wu,Guihai Chen*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: FedRAG is a federated RAG system that enables secure, efficient integration of domain-specific knowledge across institutions without violating privacy regulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of data silos in cross-institutional collaboration due to strict privacy regulations by enabling federated retrieval-augmented generation.

Method: The FedRAG framework employs a Scrambled Distributed Attention protocol that utilizes feature scrambling and token permutation for dynamic computation delegation among nodes, while preserving data privacy.

Result: FedRAG achieves less than 0.1% degradation in model utility and a 62 times latency reduction compared to traditional secure systems.

Conclusion: The proposed federated RAG framework allows efficient cross-institutional collaboration by significantly enhancing data privacy while maintaining high performance.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) empowers LLMs with external knowledge, making cross-institutional domain-specific knowledge base integration a highly promising deployment paradigm. Despite this potential, strict privacy regulations create severe "data silos" that obstruct such collaboration. Building federated RAG systems requires distributed inference, but the Transformer's self-attention mechanism fundamentally conflicts with this by mandating cross-node access to distributed Key-Value caches. To address this challenge, we present FedRAG, a high-throughput, privacy-preserving federated RAG framework. At its core is a novel Scrambled Distributed Attention protocol that utilizes numerically stable feature scrambling and token permutation. By dynamically delegating scrambled computations to collaborating nodes, our system successfully decouples attention execution from data localization without exposing plaintext. Crucially, our approach requires no specialized hardware or model retraining, circumventing the prohibitive latency and communication overheads of cryptographic solutions while robustly defending against intermediate state inversion attacks. Extensive evaluations demonstrate our framework preserves negligible (<0.1\%) model utility degradation and achieves up to a 62$\times$ latency reduction over existing secure baselines, sustaining practical, human-reading throughput for cross-institutional knowledge synergy.

</details>


### [453] [EnThM: Energy Theft Mitigation in Smart Grids using Hierarchical Verification of Metering Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24951)
*Tapadyoti Banerjee,Pabitra Mitra,Dipanwita Roy Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper proposes EnThM, a scheme for real-time electricity theft mitigation in smart grids, focusing on cryptographic security and verification of metering data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle the significant threat of electricity theft in modern smart grid systems, which compromises their reliability and efficiency.

Method: The EnThM scheme utilizes a hierarchical structure to verify metering data authenticity through statistical modeling of power usage and rule-based checks, considering consumption variations.

Result: The method demonstrates high accuracy and efficient implementation when tested on benchmark consumption data.

Conclusion: The EnThM scheme offers a robust solution for real-time power theft mitigation in smart grids, enhancing overall network operation.

Abstract: The advent of digital technologies has revolutionized traditional power distribution networks, transforming them into smart grids that are more reliable, efficient, and sustainable. Despite these advancements, electricity theft remains a significant threat to the effective operation of large electrical networks. To address this issue, we propose EnThM, a lightweight and communication-efficient scheme for real-time mitigation of power theft in smart grid systems. Our approach uses the hierarchical structure of the smart grid infrastructure to verify the authenticity of the metering data at multiple levels of the power distribution network. Our work focuses primarily on issues related to cryptographic security. The verification process involves statistically modeling the cumulative averages of the power usage data and applying rule-based checks on the aggregated power consumption at each level, while accounting for seasonal and daily consumption variations. The proposed method has been tested on benchmark consumption data, yielding high accuracy, efficient implementation, and real-time applicability.

</details>


### [454] [Proof of Useful Attestation: A Consensus Primitive for Attestation-Native Chains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25844)
*Stefan Stefanović*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces Proof of Useful Attestation (PoUA) to ensure validators on Proof of Stake chains are incentivized to correctly handle attestations, addressing issues of selective censorship that affect economic activities beyond mere token transfers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the problem of validators in Proof of Stake chains earning equal fees regardless of their attestation accuracy, which poses risks for chains reliant on attestations.

Method: PoUA integrates attestation handling into consensus weighting by linking validator vote weight to bonded stake and a reputation scalar derived from valid attestation work, accompanied by a series of defenses against Sybil attacks and grinding.

Result: Proved that the cost for adversaries to inflate reputations in the PoUA framework is significantly higher (4x to 10x) than in standard PoS systems, providing a formal economic foundation against manipulation.

Conclusion: The proposed mechanism encourages correct attestation handling among validators, offers layered defenses against attacks, and maintains Byzantine fault tolerance and liveness in the network, though further cryptographic proofs are needed.

Abstract: Validators on generic Proof of Stake chains earn the same fees whether they handle attestation work correctly or selectively censor it. For chains whose main activity is moving tokens around, that indifference is fine. For chains whose primary economic activity is recording attestations (content provenance, AI-output attribution, threshold-signed credentials, supply-chain receipts), the indifference becomes a problem.
  Proof of Useful Attestation (PoUA) makes attestation handling first-class in the consensus weighting itself. Validator vote weight is the product of bonded stake and a reputation scalar in [r_min, r_max] that accumulates from valid attestation work. The reputation update is additive, fee-weighted, non-transferable, and capped per epoch. We prove a cost-to-grind floor (Lemma 1): under chain-wide adaptive burn fraction tau_burn, the non-recoverable cost an adversary pays to inflate reputation by Delta_r is bounded below by tau_burn * Delta_r / (eta * alpha_eff). Under the recommended v0 calibration (r_max/r_min in [4, 10]), the cost premium against a capital adversary is 4x to 10x over equivalent pure-stake PoS at steady state.
  The paper specifies the mechanism, six layered Sybil and grinding defenses, empirical Monte Carlo strategy-search across the full layered defense, and grinding detectors with explicit threshold derivations. It is a mechanism-design proposal with a formal economic floor and inherited BFT safety and liveness, not a complete cryptographic security proof. This release incorporates feedback from Jiangshan Yu (University of Sydney) and Marko Vukolić (Bitcoin Scaling Labs).

</details>


### [455] [MemMark: State-Evolution Attribution Watermarking for Agent Long-Term Memory Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25002)
*Haobo Zhang,Xutao Mao,Guangyuan Dong,Ziwei Li,Xuanbo Su,Kaijie Chen,Jing Yang,Zheng Lin*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: MemMark introduces a watermarking technique for memory-backed agents that ensures safety and utility of state evolution through cryptographic commitments, achieving high F1 retention and effective payload recovery under various attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide robust attribution for memory-backed agents even when traditional metadata and logs are absent, especially in scenarios involving leaked or migrated snapshots.

Method: MemMark embeds a proprietary signal into memory-write decisions using distribution-preserving sampling and cryptographic commitments, ensuring reproducibility without relying on mutable elements.

Result: MemMark maintains 99.6% F1 and a minor BLEU-1 change, with substantial entropy in decision-making, and recovers a complete 40-bit payload from snapshots while effectively distinguishing tampering during attacks.

Conclusion: MemMark demonstrates that durable attribution for agents' long-term memory can be achieved without compromising utility or relying on vulnerable provenance records.

Abstract: Memory-backed agents need provenance that can survive leaked or migrated snapshots, where logs, visible outputs, and trusted metadata may be absent. We propose MemMark, a state-evolution attribution watermark that embeds an owner-controlled signal into latent memory-write decisions. At each internal LLM call, MemMark samples among admissible candidates using keyed, distribution-preserving selection, and records cryptographic commitments with signed session anchors and reveal evidence. This makes attribution depend on reproducible backend behavior rather than mutable provenance fields. Across A-Mem and Graphiti on LoCoMo, with three LLM backbones, MemMark preserves memory utility: Overall F1 retains 99.6% of the unwatermarked baseline, while BLEU-1 changes by +0.2%. It also provides usable carrier capacity, with 1.16, 1.14, and 1.26 bits of mean entropy for update-target, link-target, and semantic-realization decisions. In the snapshot-only R3 setting, MemMark recovers the full 40-bit payload from final snapshots, while wrong-key verification remains near chance. Under nine memory-lifecycle attacks, verification distinguishes tampering, evidence deletion, and partial payload recovery. These results show that robust snapshot-only attribution is feasible for long-term agent memory without surviving traces, trusted metadata, or utility-degrading.

</details>


### [456] [Securing High-Performance Data Transfers: Implementing AES Encryption in RDMA Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25026)
*Erik Bångsbo,Zakaria Hersi,Anna Benktson,Stefan Holmgren,Romaric Duvignau*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper proposes integrating AES-128 encryption into Remote Direct Memory Access (RDMA) systems by implementing it on a Tofino switch to enhance security without sacrificing performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address security vulnerabilities in RDMA caused by bypassing traditional CPU-mediated mechanisms in untrusted environments.

Method: The paper integrates AES-128 encryption directly in the data plane of a programmable Tofino switch using the P4 programming language, offloading encryption from the CPU.

Result: The system achieves throughput rates of 0.37 Gbps for 16-byte packets, 0.76 Gbps for 32-byte packets, 1.83 Gbps for 64-byte packets, and 1.9 Gbps for 128-byte packets.

Conclusion: The findings confirm that secure, high-throughput RDMA communication is feasible using programmable network hardware.

Abstract: Remote Direct Memory Access (RDMA) is a key enabler of high-performance systems, offering low latency, high throughput, and reduced CPU overhead by allowing direct memory-to-memory transfers between machines. However, its design bypasses traditional CPU-mediated security mechanisms, introducing critical vulnerabilities in untrusted environments. This work explores the integration of RDMA and AES-128 encryption to secure data transfers without compromising performance. We implement encryption directly within the data plane of a programmable Tofino switch using the P4 programming language. By offloading encryption from the CPU to the switch, our design preserves RDMA's performance benefits while addressing its security shortcomings. Experimental results show that the system achieves throughput of 0.37 Gbps for 16-byte packets, 0.76 Gbps for 32-byte packets, 1.83 Gbps for 64-byte packets, and 1.9 Gbps for 128-byte packets. These findings demonstrate the feasibility of secure, high-throughput RDMA communication using programmable network hardware.

</details>


### [457] [Security in the Fine-Tuning Lifecycle of Large Language Models: Threats, Defenses,Evaluation, and Future Directions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25073)
*Wenjuan Li,Yitao Liu,Runze Chen,Rajkumar Buyya*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper surveys fine-tuning security of Large Language Models (LLMs), offering a lifecycle-based framework for analyzing security threats and their defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The increasing sophistication of attacks on fine-tuning processes necessitates a comprehensive framework to understand and defend against these threats.

Method: Attacks and defenses are categorized into pre-tuning, during-tuning, and post-tuning phases, with evaluations conducted under controlled conditions to compare strategies across the lifecycle.

Result: Findings indicate that attack effectiveness varies significantly by model type and phase, with certain attacks losing potency in modern LLMs, while some defenses fail to generalize across phases.

Conclusion: The paper identifies critical open problems in LLM fine-tuning security and suggests future research directions to address these issues.

Abstract: Background: Fine-tuning is central to adapting pre-trained Large Language Models (LLMs) to downstream tasks, but its reliance on training data, parameter updates, and reusable components opens entry points for attackers. Threats have evolved from data poisoning and weight tampering to agent manipulation and interface exploitation, yet existing reviews lack a unified framework spanning the full fine-tuning lifecycle. Objective: This paper presents a systematic survey of LLM fine-tuning security and establishes a lifecycle-based framework for comparing attacks and defenses, complemented by unified empirical evaluation. Methods: We divide attack and defense mechanisms into three phases by intervention timing: pre-tuning, during-tuning, and post-tuning. Within each phase, strategies are reviewed and contrasted to expose their evolution and limitations. Representative methods are then evaluated under a unified model, hardware, and protocol setup, with cross-phase experiments pairing attacks and defenses from different phases. Results: Attack effectiveness is highly model-dependent and non-monotonic with scale: weight-editing attacks effective on earlier models lose impact on modern open-source LLMs; cross-lingual backdoor transfer, reported as near-perfect at larger scales, fails entirely on tested 1B-4B models; and purely benign samples can compromise safety alignment in instruction-tuned models. Single-phase defenses rarely generalize across phases, and defense effectiveness depends jointly on model architecture and alignment state. Conclusion: We identify key open problems (configuration-robust defense, cross-phase defense composition, and embedding-space attacks beyond behavioral assumptions) and propose concrete future research directions.

</details>


### [458] [Decoupling Reentrancy Protection from Smart Contract Implementation Logic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25207)
*Shashank Joshi,Wojciech Golab*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Sentinel is a novel proxy-based approach designed to mitigate reentrancy vulnerabilities in decentralized applications (DApps) by integrating reentrancy logic into the proxy layer, achieving complete security coverage and outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The persistent threat of reentrancy attacks in DApps, exploiting EVM vulnerabilities, necessitates more robust, deployable solutions beyond existing detection and guard mechanisms.

Method: Sentinel employs a proxy-based model that integrates reentrancy logic directly into the proxy layer to intercept calls to contracts, featuring dual operational modes for enhanced security and gas optimization.

Result: Sentinel provides 100% security coverage across four major reentrancy attack categories and demonstrates a 40% improvement over existing solutions based on an evaluation involving 70 vulnerable smart contracts.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses reentrancy vulnerabilities in a comprehensive manner, outperforming current methodologies and ensuring safer decentralized application interactions.

Abstract: Reentrancy attacks remain a persistent threat to decentralized applications (DApps), with malicious actors siphoning around 80M USD from the DApp ecosystem last year by exploiting EVM's inter-contract message-passing semantics. Existing research focuses primarily on detection, relying on known attack patterns, and fails to provide deployable solutions that eliminate the vulnerability. Traditional reentrancy guards are similarly limited, offering incomplete coverage across attack variations and lacking robustness against complex DApp interactions. In this paper, we introduce Sentinel, a novel proxy-based approach that mitigates reentrancy vulnerabilities in a type-agnostic way by integrating reentrancy logic directly into the proxy layer, intercepting all calls to the underlying implementation contract. Key features include a dual-mode operational system offering both a gas-optimized internal guard and a high-security external lock registry for cross-contract reentrancy prevention. The proxy also intelligently handles static calls, enabling safe view-function execution while protecting against Read-Only Reentrancy (ROR) attacks. Through rigorous evaluation on a dataset of 70 vulnerable smart contracts, Sentinel achieves 100% security coverage across four major reentrancy attack categories, outperforming existing solutions by over 40%

</details>


### [459] [Heimdall: Formally Verified Automated Migration of Legacy eBPF Programs to Rust](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25411)
*Vishnu Asutosh Dasu,Monika Santra,Md Rafi Ur Rashid,Ashish Kumar,Saeid Tizpaz-Niari,Gang Tan*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper presents Heimdall, a system that automates the migration of eBPF programs from C to Rust, ensuring safety and correctness in the process.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address source-level bugs in eBPF programs that are not checked by the kernel verifier, which can lead to data corruption and security issues.

Method: Heimdall translates eBPF programs to Aya Rust using large language models and fixes compilation errors while ensuring safety through static analysis and verifying program equivalence via symbolic execution.

Result: Heimdall achieves a 94.1% success rate in producing formally proven-equivalent translations across 102 eBPF programs, demonstrating its effectiveness and safety guarantees.

Conclusion: Heimdall is the first automated system that ensures memory-safe migration of eBPF programs with formal guarantees that the translated programs maintain their original behavior.

Abstract: Extended Berkeley Packet Filter (eBPF) programs are kernel extensions used for networking, observability, and security enforcement in the Linux kernel. The in-kernel eBPF verifier checks low-level memory safety and termination on eBPF programs, but it does not enforce many higher-level source-level properties, such as initialization discipline, schema consistency, or error handling. We document six classes of source-level bugs that compile, pass the kernel verifier, and can silently corrupt data, leak previously traced events to userspace, or yield incorrect enforcement outcomes. Among these, we identify previously unreported information leaks in ten open-source eBPF programs whose ring-buffer or stack-resident event records carry fully decodable prior traced events, including user-identifying paths and recurring kernel-text return addresses sufficient to recover the KASLR slide on every event, into userspace. To harden such verifier-accepted buggy programs and support safe migration, we present Heimdall, an automated pipeline that uses large language models to translate legacy libbpf C programs to Aya Rust. Heimdall iteratively repairs compilation and kernel-verifier failures, rejects unsafe escape hatches in Rust-Aya with a static analysis safety engine, and proves per-program equivalence to the original via symbolic execution and Z3-based equivalence checking. Across 102 eBPF programs, Heimdall produces 96 formally proven-equivalent translations (94.1%). Heimdall is the first system to automate memory-safe-language migration of production eBPF programs with per-program formal guarantees that the migration preserves observable behavior.

</details>


### [460] [Referential Security as a New Paradigm for AI Evaluations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25673)
*Dan Ristea,Vasilios Mavroudis*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper proposes referential security as a paradigm for evaluating AI systems, ensuring stability in identifiers despite ongoing modifications, leading to reliable audits and safety evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation lies in the instability of identifying AI systems as they continuously evolve, leading to challenges in linking evaluations and regulatory decisions to specific artifacts.

Method: The authors propose referential security, focusing on the empirically verifiable properties of model identity, which separates referential stability from substantive security claims.

Result: This new framework enables reproducible evaluations, maintains validity in longitudinal audits, and establishes equivalence across different AI system providers.

Conclusion: By grounding evaluations in verifiable artifacts, the approach enhances the reliability of safety audits and regulatory findings throughout the lifecycle of dynamic AI systems.

Abstract: Security evaluations inherently depend on stable identifiers. Any finding, audit, or regulatory decision must remain attached to the specific artifact it pertains to. Continuously updated artificial intelligence systems violate this core assumption, with public model designations remaining static while underlying weights, prompts, retrieval mechanisms, misuse classifiers, inference settings, and serving infrastructures undergo unannounced modifications. Consequently, current evaluations frequently apply to superficial labels rather than identifiable and distinct systems.
  To resolve this, we propose referential security as a new paradigm for AI evaluation. The fundamental security question extends beyond whether a model is safe to whether subsequent parties can conclusively determine which system a specific safety claim addressed. This approach reframes model identity as an empirically verifiable property and separates referential stability from the substantive security claims it conditions. This framework brings tractability to three critical workflows that current practices handle poorly. Specifically, it enables reproducible evaluation, longitudinal audit validity, and cross-provider equivalence. By grounding these evaluations in verifiable artifacts, our approach ensures that safety audits and regulatory findings maintain their empirical utility across the operational lifecycle of dynamic systems.

</details>


### [461] [Ecosystem-Driven Privacy Exposure in Mobile Gaming Apps: A Configuration-Aware Empirical Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25713)
*Bakheet Aljedaani*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This study analyzes privacy exposure in Android mobile gaming apps, revealing that SDK ecosystem complexity and architectural choices significantly affect privacy risks, surpassing traditional permission-based assessments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inadequate understanding of privacy risks in mobile gaming apps due to reliance on third-party SDKs beyond traditional permission frameworks.

Method: Conducted a static analysis of 41 Android gaming apps assessing permissions, configurations, exported components, and SDK complexity using statistical tests.

Result: Findings indicate that privacy exposure is more closely linked to ecosystem architecture than to permission requests, with child-oriented games showing significant exposure despite fewer permissions.

Conclusion: Emphasizes the need for configuration and ecosystem-aware evaluation methodologies in assessing privacy risks in mobile software, beyond common permission-centric approaches.

Abstract: Mobile gaming apps increasingly rely on third-party Software Development Kits SDKs for advertising, analytics, attribution, and user engagement, potentially introducing privacy exposure beyond traditional permission based risks. Existing studies have largely focused on permissions or isolated tracking behaviors, providing only a partial understanding of privacy exposure in modern mobile ecosystems. This study presents a configuration aware empirical assessment of privacy exposure in Android mobile gaming apps by examining permissions, manifest level configurations, exported components, and SDK ecosystem complexity across children-oriented and general-audience games. A systematic static analysis was conducted on 41 widely deployed Android mobile gaming apps collected from the Google Play ecosystem. The analysis incorporated SDK categorisation and statistical evaluation using Spearman correlation, Mann Whitney U, and Chi square testing. The results revealed that privacy exposure is strongly associated with ecosystem-level architectural decisions rather than permission requests alone. Child-oriented games frequently demonstrated exposure conditions comparable to general-audience apps despite sometimes requesting fewer sensitive permissions. Furthermore, larger and more diverse SDK ecosystems were significantly associated with elevated privacy exposure levels, while advertising-oriented SDKs showed strong association with high exposure classifications. These findings highlight the limitations of permission-centric assessment approaches and emphasize the importance of configuration aware and ecosystem-aware privacy evaluation methodologies for modern mobile software systems.

</details>


### [462] [Efficient and Privacy-Preserving Distribution Statistics Analytics on Mobile Spatial Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25791)
*Xuhao Ren,Mingyang Zhao,Ruichen Zhang,Liehuang Zhu,Dusit Niyato,Bin Xiao*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper presents eSpat-B and eSpat+, systems designed for efficient, privacy-preserving statistical analysis of mobile spatial data, addressing the challenges of privacy and overhead in mobile environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the privacy challenges in distributed statistical analysis of spatial data generated from mobile devices, while minimizing computational and communication overhead.

Method: Introduces eSpat-B using non-colluding servers and an improved distributed point functions with octree partitioning, and eSpat+ utilizing a K-D tree for spatial partitioning with incremental DPF for statistical analysis.

Result: The proposed systems achieve a significant reduction in computation and communication overhead while maintaining 100% accuracy, verified through theoretical analysis and real-world data experiments.

Conclusion: The proposed schemes efficiently protect data privacy and enhance the performance of statistical analysis in mobile computing contexts.

Abstract: With the rapid development of mobile computing technology, massive amounts of spatial data are continuously generated from various mobile terminals and sensing devices, such as smartphones, connected vehicles, and drones. Performing efficient distributed statistical analysis on this data is crucial for real-time mobile computing applications. However, the constrained and dynamic nature of mobile environments exacerbates the privacy challenge: centralizing sensitive data for analysis risks severe privacy leaks, while existing privacy-preserving techniques often introduce excessive overhead or inaccuracies In this paper, we design, implement, and evaluate the first system that supports efficient and privacy-preserving distribution statistics analysis for mobile spatial data. First, we propose eSpat-B, which leverages two non-colluding servers and a newly designed improved distributed point functions (DPF) with octree partitioning. Furthermore, considering the frequent updates of spatial data, we propose another more efficient scheme, eSpat+. The core idea of this scheme is to utilize a K-Dimensional tree for spatial partitioning, combine it with incremental DPF for performing statistics analysis, and design an efficient update algorithm. Security analysis demonstrates that our schemes effectively protect data privacy throughout the statistical process. Theoretical analysis and experimental results on real-world mobile trajectory datasets demonstrate that our proposed schemes achieve a reduction of approximately 1.2* in computation overhead, 20* in communication overhead, and maintain 100% accuracy.

</details>


### [463] [SAMark: A Self-Anchored Text Watermarking with Paragraph-Level Paraphrase Robustness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25796)
*Jiahao Huo,Wenjie Qu,Yibo Yan,Kening Zheng,Jiaheng Zhang,Xuming Hu,Philip S. Yu,Mingxun Zhou*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: SAMark is a self-anchored watermarking framework designed to enhance robustness against paragraph-level paraphrasing attacks by decoupling sentence order dependence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the vulnerability of semantic-level watermarking to paragraph-level paraphrasing, which disrupts watermark signals by changing sentence order.

Method: SAMark introduces a green region in semantic space independent of sentence order, employs a multi-channel hyperbolic scoring mechanism for better detectability, and a diversity-aware filtering strategy for managing semantic redundancy.

Result: SAMark achieves up to 90.2% true positive rate at a 1% false positive rate in paragraph-level paraphrasing attacks, surpassing previous methods by over 30% on average.

Conclusion: SAMark effectively balances robustness against attacks with text generation quality, overcoming limitations of prior watermarking techniques.

Abstract: Semantic-level watermarking (SWM) improves robustness against text modifications by treating sentences as the basic unit. However, robustness to paragraph-level paraphrasing remains difficult because such attacks globally disrupt watermark signals by changing sentence order. In this work, we propose SAMark, a self-anchored watermarking framework that removes the dependency on sentence order by establishing a step-independent green region in semantic space. To improve detectability, we introduce a multi-channel hyperbolic scoring mechanism that amplifies watermark signals while suppressing noise from weakly aligned candidates. We further propose a diversity-aware filtering strategy that combines hard filtering with soft regularization, extending beyond simple n-gram repetition filters to address semantic redundancy. Experimental results show that SAMark achieves up to 90.2% TP@FP1% under typical paragraph-level paraphrasing attacks, outperforming the strongest prior baseline by more than 30% on average, while maintaining generation quality competitive with unwatermarked text and breaking the robustness-quality trade-off that limits prior methods.

</details>


### [464] ["What is the Problem Space?" Defining Host-space Adversarial Perturbations against Network Intrusion Detection Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25822)
*Miel Verkerken,Laurens D'hooge,Bruno Volckaert,Filip De Turck,Giovanni Apruzzese*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper critiques the effectiveness of ML-based Network Intrusion Detection Systems (ML-NIDS), highlighting a novel focus on 'host-space perturbations' that attackers can realistically execute, suggesting a need for reevaluation of current security measures against such tactics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses gaps in the existing research on adversarial perturbations applied to ML-NIDS, specifically under the constraints of real-world attackers who can only manipulate hosts they control.

Method: Conducted a systematic literature review of 316 papers and executed experiments on established benchmarks and a real-world network to demonstrate the effects of host-space perturbations.

Result: Findings reveal that a trivial change, such as modifying a single character in an attack command, can allow malicious activities to bypass detection by ML-NIDS designed to catch SSH-bruteforcing attempts.

Conclusion: The research concludes that the security of ML-NIDS must be re-assessed in light of the demonstrated vulnerabilities associated with host-space perturbations.

Abstract: Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) are now increasingly leveraging Machine Learning (ML) techniques to detect malicious network activities. Numerous papers have scrutinized the security of ML-based NIDS (ML-NIDS) by testing them against various attacks involving adversarial perturbations. The findings were oftentimes worrying: by making imperceptible changes to a given input, powerful ML models would be bypassed. In this context, we took a step back and wondered: where (i.e., in what "space") have these perturbations been applied?
  We argue that real-world adversaries can apply adversarial perturbations only by operating on the hosts they can control -- a concept which we define as _host-space perturbations_. To some, such an observation may seem trivial. And yet, through a systematic literature review (n=316), we found that prior work applied perturbations by manipulating pre-collected datapoints (e.g., a packet _captured by the router_, or a network flow _analysed by the ML-NIDS_). Such operations, while not impossible, may be outside the reach of an attacker who can only control some (unprivileged) hosts in a network. Hence, to demonstrate how to craft host-space perturbations and study some of their effects, we experimented on well-known benchmarks and a real-world network. We show that ML-NIDS that can detect the SSH-bruteforcing attempts launched via a given command string cannot detect any attempt launched by changing _a single character_ of such a string. We then examined how such a minuscule change in the "problem space" (i.e., the attacker's host) can lead to devastating effects on the "feature space". We derive lessons learned on how to practically assess host-space perturbations. Our stance is that the security of ML-NIDS should be re-assessed.

</details>


### [465] [TTPrint: Evidence-Grounded TTP Extraction via Diverge-then-Converge Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25836)
*Yutong Cheng,Changze Li,Raihan Sultan Pasha Basuki,Qian Cui,Wei Ding,Peng Gao*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper presents TTPrint, a novel method for extracting MITRE ATT&CK techniques from cyber threat intelligence reports, achieving improved recall and precision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extracting techniques from CTI reports is challenging due to the need for high recall and precision, which existing methods fail to balance.

Method: TTPrint uses a diverge-then-converge design: first extracting techniques broadly from reports, then rigorously verifying and localizing them based on evidence and MITRE definitions.

Result: TTPrint achieves macro-F1 scores of 76.48% and 87.39% on the TRAM-Clean and TTPrint-Bench datasets, significantly outperforming existing baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed method enhances the extraction of techniques from CTI reports, demonstrating high generalizability and providing valuable resources for further research.

Abstract: Extracting MITRE ATT&CK techniques from cyber threat intelligence (CTI) reports is an open-set, multi-label problem requiring both high recall (not missing techniques) and high precision (not hallucinating unsupported ones). Existing methods--rule-based, supervised, and LLM-based--struggle to achieve both: rule-based and supervised approaches lack generalizability across diverse attack descriptions, while LLM-based approaches that couple candidate generation and validation within a single inference step suffer from limited recall and precision simultaneously. We propose TTPrint, which addresses this challenge through a diverge-then-converge design inspired by how human analysts work: first extracting broadly, then verifying rigorously. In the divergent phase, reports are decomposed into atomic behaviors and candidate techniques are proposed broadly. A deterministic span localization stage then anchors each candidate to a specific evidence window in the source text. A convergent verification stage retains only candidates supported by both the localized evidence and the authoritative MITRE definition. We contribute two evaluation resources--a cleaned TRAM benchmark (TRAM-Clean) and a new annotated dataset (TTPrint-Bench)--to address known annotation noise in existing benchmarks and elevate the task to document-level TTP extraction. On TRAM-Clean and TTPrint-Bench, TTPrint achieves 76.48% and 87.39% macro-F1 respectively, outperforming the leading baseline by 63.5% and 29.4%. A multi-backbone analysis across six LLMs and a threshold sensitivity study further demonstrate generalizability across model choices and provide practical guidance for parameter selection.

</details>


### [466] [Broken Object Level Authorization in the Wild: An Empirical Taxonomy from 100+ Bug Bounty Disclosures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25865)
*Bandana Kaur*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper provides a large-scale empirical analysis of Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA) through the evaluation of 200 bug bounty reports, revealing insights into its prevalence, classification, and implications for API security.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of empirical studies on BOLA, a critical API security vulnerability, and to provide concrete insights from real-world bug bounty disclosures.

Method: Analyzed 200 HackerOne bug reports tagged IDOR or Improper Access Control, applying a three-criterion inclusion filter to classify 107 reports using an LLM-assisted schema-completion procedure against a six-family BOLA taxonomy.

Result: Confirmed that 78.5% of analyzed reports were in-scope BOLA, with Action-Level Object BOLA and Direct Object Reference BOLA as the predominant families; identified a significant discrepancy in tagging accuracy on platforms like HackerOne.

Conclusion: This research emphasizes the need for improved API security testing protocols, developer education, and refined OWASP guidance based on empirical evidence of BOLA vulnerabilities.

Abstract: Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA) is consistently ranked the most critical API security vulnerability, yet the existing literature remains almost entirely conceptual. This paper presents one of the first large-scale empirical analyses of BOLA in publicly disclosed bug bounty reports. We constructed a reproducible sampling frame of 200 HackerOne disclosures tagged IDOR or Improper Access Control (2021-2026) and applied a three-criterion inclusion filter, yielding 107 fully classified reports. Classification used an LLM-assisted schema-completion procedure under constrained, human-adjudicated criteria against a six-family BOLA taxonomy. Of 107 classified reports, 84 (78.5%) were confirmed in-scope BOLA. Action-Level Object BOLA, defined by unauthorized state-changing actions on another user's objects, accounts for 41.7% of confirmed cases and emerges alongside Direct Object Reference BOLA as one of the two dominant families observed in the dataset. This shows a pattern historically underrepresented in practitioner guidance. Approximately 21.5% of classified reports are out-of-scope under strict criteria, indicating that tag-counting on platforms like HackerOne significantly overstates the BOLA-specific signal. We report distributions across family, action type, authorization direction, industry sector, identifier format, and exploit mechanism. Key secondary findings include an 11.9% rate of vertical (user-to-admin) privilege failures and systematic exploitation of GraphQL Global IDs across major platforms. Findings have direct implications for API security testing protocols, developer education, and OWASP guidance.

</details>


### [467] [Capability and Robustness Cannot Both Be Free: An Information-Theoretic Bound for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25889)
*Jianwei Tai*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper establishes a theoretical upper bound on the trade-off between robustness and capability in Vision-Language-Action models under adversarial perturbations, demonstrating the limited scope for improvements without sacrificing performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models encounter severe performance decline when facing adversarial attacks, raising the question of whether there is a theoretical limit to the trade-off between robustness and clean accuracy.

Method: The authors use the Data Processing Inequality to prove that the sum of mutual information between policy action and oracle action (capability) and the preserved mutual information under adversarial perturbation (robustness) is constrained by a fixed budget determined by task entropy and adversarial channel capacity.

Result: The upper bound on the robustness and capability trade-off is validated across various models, revealing that current models have limited room for improvement in robustness, with a specific reduction in the available budget for OpenVLA.

Conclusion: The findings emphasize the need for encoder-specific approaches in evaluating defense strategies and provide tools for standardized comparisons in defense research.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are increasingly deployed on real robots, where each predicted action is executed and each failure carries a safety cost. They reach high success rates on clean inputs but collapse under small adversarial perturbations. A $16/255$ PGD attack on OpenVLA-7B drops LIBERO success from above $95\%$ to under $5\%$. Empirical defenses recover some robustness at a cost in clean accuracy, but the literature does not say whether the trade-off has a theoretical floor. We prove that it does. For any VLA policy with discrete actions, the sum of capability (mutual information between policy action and oracle action) and robustness (mutual information preserved under adversarial perturbation, net of trivial channel leakage) is upper-bounded by a policy-independent budget: task entropy plus adversarial channel capacity. The proof is two applications of the Data Processing Inequality plus MI non-negativity. The pixel-level bound is loose on current models ($\sim 10^3$ nats), but an encoder-specific corollary restricts the channel to the policy-relevant subspace, reducing the budget from $\sim 5{,}000$ to $\sim 31$ nats on OpenVLA; the policy already consumes $\sim 24\%$ of this tighter budget, leaving limited room for simultaneous robustness improvement. We validate the bound across $252$ closed-form Gaussian-VLA cells and $48$ OpenVLA-7B $\times$ LIBERO $\times$ PGD cells (zero violations). We propose encoder-specific slack as a normalized comparison axis for defense papers, and release all code, manifests, and results.

</details>


### [468] [Semantic Validation of Packer Identification Tools: Characterization, Repair, and Downstream Impact](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25923)
*Fangtian Zhong,Zhuoyun Qian,Mengfei Ren,Yili Jiang,Jiaqi Huang,Yunming Pang,Xiuzhen Cheng*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper introduces a semantic validation framework to improve the reliability of packer identification tools in malware analysis by detecting and rectifying semantic faults.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the issue of semantic inaccuracies in packer identification tools, which can lead to ineffective unpacking and unreliable analysis results.

Method: The authors propose using unpackers as executable semantic contracts for validation. They develop a systematic pipeline to detect, localize, and repair faults in packer identification tools, without needing manually labeled ground truth.

Result: The empirical study on eleven open-source and six proprietary tools revealed widespread semantic bugs attributed to incomplete signatures and unstable heuristics. Post-repair, packer identification coverage increased by up to 58.6%, and malware classification improved by over 13.6% on average.

Conclusion: The study underscores the necessity of semantic validation for packer identification tools to ensure the reliability of malware analysis processes.

Abstract: Packer identification tools are a critical foundation of malware analysis, directly affecting unpacking, behavioral analysis, malware classification, and threat attribution. However, their semantic correctness is rarely validated. In practice, a tool may return a plausible packer label that is nevertheless semantically wrong, leading to failed unpacking and unreliable downstream analysis. This paper presents a semantic validation framework for testing and repairing packer identification tools. Our key idea is to use unpackers as executable semantic contracts. If a tool predicts a packer family, the corresponding unpacker should recover analyzable program content. This enables automatic test oracles without requiring manually labeled ground truth. Building on this idea, we develop a systematic pipeline for detecting, localizing, and repairing semantic faults in existing packer identification tools. We then conduct the first large-scale empirical study of semantic bugs in eleven open-source packer identification tools and six proprietary VirusTotal tools. Our results reveal that semantic bugs are widespread and recurring, largely due to incomplete signatures and unstable heuristic logic. After repair, packer identification coverage improves by up to 58.6%, and downstream malware classification performance improves by more than 13.6% on average. These findings show that semantic validation of packer identification tools is essential for building trustworthy malware analysis pipelines.

</details>


### [469] [Building an Adversarial Malware Dataset by Family and Type: Generation, Evasion, and Poisoning Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25937)
*David Košťál,Martin Jureček*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A dataset of adversarial malware samples was created to evaluate the robustness of malware classification systems, demonstrating high evasion rates and the impact of data poisoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a resource for research on adversarial malware and to investigate the vulnerabilities of malware classification systems to adversarial attacks.

Method: Constructed adversarial PE files from the RawMal-TF collection, producing family- and type-labelled sets, and evaluated evasion rates against the EMBER classifier, also testing data poisoning effects by injecting mislabelled samples.

Result: Achieved evasion rates of 98.35% and 92.20% against EMBER for the family and type-labelled samples respectively; data poisoning increased evasion rates significantly from 26.1% to 92.8%.

Conclusion: The dataset supports future research on adversarial malware and the evaluation of machine-learning detection systems against various attack vectors.

Abstract: We present a dataset of adversarial malware samples derived from the public RawMal-TF collection of real-world malware binaries. Using a suite of adversarial malware generators, we construct two sets of adversarial PE files: 44,347 family-labelled samples and 33,596 type-labelled samples, achieving evasion rates of 98.35 % and 92.20 % against the EMBER classifier, respectively. Each adversarial binary is accompanied by detailed metadata, including EMBER scores and VirusTotal classifications. We further demonstrate the susceptibility of malware classification pipelines to data poisoning attacks through a series of training experiments. Injecting fully mislabelled adversarial samples representing only 0.5 % of the training data in the family-labelled dataset increases the evasion rate against the re-trained classifier from 26.1 % to 92.8 %. The dataset is publicly released to facilitate future research on adversarial malware, poisoning attacks, and the robustness of machine-learning-based malware detection systems.

</details>


### [470] [Shielded but Lightweight: Building Practical Confidential Containers with ARM CCA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26018)
*Liantao Song,Yiming Zhang,Fengwei Zhang,Yan Ding,Bin Zhou,Jie Yu,Yusong Tan*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Fasco introduces a lightweight confidential container runtime enhancing security and performance in cloud-native environments by using ARM CCA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for secure multi-tenant environments for confidential containers due to the limitations of existing microVM-based architectures.

Method: Fasco utilizes ARM Confidential Compute Architecture to create independent Container Realms and a System Realm for effective resource management and isolation.

Result: Prototype implementation shows reduced startup latency and performance overhead compared to existing architectures, with a minimal trusted computing base (TCB).

Conclusion: Fasco effectively addresses the challenges of performance and security in confidential container deployment.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of cloud-native technologies has created an urgent need for security. Currently, confidential containers are increasingly deployed in multi-tenant environments. Existing confidential container designs mainly adopt a microVM-based architecture. Although this approach improves inter-container isolation, its complex software stack leads to high startup latency and significant resource overhead, making it unsuitable for short-lived container workloads. In this paper, we propose Fasco, a lightweight confidential container runtime based on the ARM Confidential Compute Architecture (CCA). Fasco directly instantiates each container as an independent Container Realm, leveraging CCA's hardware-enforced isolation to ensure the confidentiality and integrity of application data inside the container. In addition, Fasco introduces a dedicated System Realm to provide system services and resource management for container realms. Through exception forwarding and shared buffers, Fasco ensures isolation among different container realms. We have implemented a prototype of Fasco and evaluated its performance on ARMv8 hardware. Experimental results show that Fasco reduces the startup latency and performance overhead of existing confidential container architectures while maintaining a small TCB.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [471] [Radio Environment Mapping with World Models for Active Measurement Control: Should Networks Dream of Optimal Control?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24028)
*Jernej Hribar,Ljupcho Milosheski,Ryoichi Shinkuma*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper presents a new approach for constructing Radio Environment Maps (REMs) using a world-model-inspired framework for active RSSI map reconstruction, outperforming traditional methods in efficiency and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance REM construction in AI-native 6G networks, addressing limitations of existing passive methods that do not optimize measurement quality.

Method: Formulates REM construction as a sequential decision-making problem, leveraging a dreaming mechanism to simulate future measurements and dynamically select optimal measurement locations.

Result: The proposed method significantly reduces Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) compared to Gaussian Process-based interpolation, achieving up to a fivefold improvement in accuracy within a limited measurement budget.

Conclusion: The findings indicate that world models offer a promising approach for efficient radio environment mapping and intelligent sensing in future 6G networks.

Abstract: Radio Environment Maps (REMs) have the potential to serve as an important enabler for intelligent modeling and control in emerging AI-native 6G networks. Despite significant progress, most REM construction methods remain passive, relying on interpolation or static uncertainty models and lacking an explicit mechanism to reason about how future measurements will affect reconstruction quality under a limited measurement budget. In this paper, we formulate REM construction as a sequential decision-making problem and propose a world-model-inspired framework for active Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI) map reconstruction. By learning an internal representation of the radio environment and employing a dreaming mechanism to simulate the impact of candidate measurements, the proposed approach actively selects measurement locations under a limited budget. Experimental results on real indoor RSSI data demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms Gaussian Process-based interpolation in the few-shot regime, achieving up to a fivefold reduction in Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) with the same number of measurements. These results highlight the potential of world models as a powerful paradigm for sample-efficient radio environment mapping and intelligent model-based sensing in 6G and beyond networks.

</details>


### [472] [LWM-CDE: A Representation Space for Wireless Data Reasoning and Transferability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24077)
*Sadjad Alikhani,Akshay Malhotra,Shahab Hamidi-Rad,Ahmed Alkhateeb*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: LWM-CDE introduces a dataset similarity framework for enhancing model transferability in wireless communication by fine-tuning dataset embeddings based on a pretrained model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve model deployment and life cycle management in wireless tasks by addressing the challenges of data similarity assessment and model transferability.

Method: A dataset similarity framework, LWM-CDE, which utilizes contrastive and geometry-shaping losses to fine-tune dataset embeddings from a pretrained wireless foundation model, resulting in a structured manifold for evaluating transferability.

Result: LWM-CDE demonstrates stronger correlation with empirical transfer performance compared to existing metrics while being computationally efficient through extensive experiments on wireless benchmarks.

Conclusion: The learned representation space enables better decision-making for dataset selection and other augmentation tasks, highlighting its versatility in wireless communication applications.

Abstract: Machine learning deployments in real-world wireless communication tasks face significant generalization challenges due to location and environment-specific signal structure, high diversity in data across different deployments, and limited availability of real-world data. Current approaches for assessing data similarity between training and inference (deployment) distributions, as well as evaluating model transferability, suffer from high computational costs and inconsistent performance, leaving critical model deployment and model life cycle management decisions without a principled foundation. To address this, we introduce a dataset similarity framework built upon the feature space of a pretrained wireless foundation model. Our method, LWM-CDE (Contrastive learning of Dataset Embedding), fine-tunes the dataset embeddings of the foundation model using a combination of contrastive and geometry-shaping losses, creating a structured manifold where distance reliably indicates transferability. Extensive experiments on wireless benchmarks show that LWM-CDE achieves stronger correlation with empirical transfer performance than existing metrics while being more computationally efficient. The learned representation space supports more effective and data-efficient decision-making for tasks like source dataset selection, label-aware augmentation, and budgeted pretraining, demonstrating its broader utility across different wireless communication applications.

</details>


### [473] [Feasibility Study of VLC-Based Collective Perception for Vehicular Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24246)
*Kosuke Nakano,Shan Lu,Takaya Yamazato*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This study investigates the integration of Visible Light Communication (VLC) in Collective Perception (CP) for V2X systems to address spectrum limitations and enhance traffic safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce traffic accidents by improving vehicle and infrastructure communication through Collective Perception, particularly addressing the limited radio spectrum available for V2X communication.

Method: The paper proposes the integration of VLC into V2X systems and evaluates its effectiveness through experiments measuring application-level delay, communication range, and robustness during driving at high speeds.

Result: Experiments show that VLC-based CP is feasible, demonstrating effective communication in both stationary and dynamic environments, which indicates VLC can help alleviate spectrum scarcity issues in V2X communications.

Conclusion: VLC presents a promising enhancement for V2X communications, particularly in Collective Perception, by providing an additional channel for data exchange and improving traffic safety.

Abstract: This study explores the use of Visible Light Communication (VLC) in Collective Perception (CP), a technology that enables vehicles and infrastructure to share sensor information to help reduce traffic accidents. Recent advances in Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication have spurred growing research interest in CP. However, in regions such as the United States and Japan, only 30 MHz of radio spectrum is allocated for V2X, which is insufficient to effectively support CP. In this paper, we propose integrating VLC into V2X systems to enhance CP, complementing the existing 5.9 GHz band for V2X communications. VLC can coexist with wireless systems that use radio waves, providing an additional optical channel for data exchange. To the best of the authors' knowledge, this is the first study to investigate VLC for CP. We evaluate the feasibility of VLC-based CP through three experiments. First, we measured the application-level delay of a VLC-based CP system in a stationary indoor environment. Next, we evaluated its communication range in a stationary outdoor setting. Finally, to assess robustness under realistic conditions, we conducted driving experiments at vehicle speeds up to 90 km/h. The results demonstrate that VLC-based CP is feasible and could serve as a promising solution to spectrum scarcity in the 5.9 GHz band for future V2X communications.

</details>


### [474] [A Learning Method for Optimal Chirp Predistortion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24256)
*Averal N. Kandala,Ali M. Niknejad*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper presents the "chart & chirp" method for one-shot VCO tuning and predistortion, outperforming conventional LMS techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide an efficient alternative to LMS-based calibration for VCO tuning curves and predistortion in radar applications.

Method: Utilizes a cycle-counting FDC for VCO tuning curve estimation and a QDAC for predistortion, with an LLSE learning process for DAC control codes based on chirp parameters.

Result: Achieved RMS FM errors of 50 and 51 kHz for 5 and 20 µs chirps, respectively, while predicting nonlinearity-induced spurs in the IF spectrum.

Conclusion: Deterministic phase error from chirp nonlinearity significantly influences IF SNDR, with measured values around 40 dB at close range and 24 dB at further distances.

Abstract: This article introduces the "chart & chirp" method of one-shot, in situ VCO tuning curve estimation, learning, and predistortion, which provides an alternative to prevailing LMS-based background calibration loops. The proposed approach utilizes a cycle-counting FDC to estimate the VCO tuning curve and a linearly interpolating QDAC to actuate the predistortion. An LLSE-based learning process generates predistorted DAC control codes for any physically achievable chirp parameters. In simulation, 50 and 51 kHz of RMS FM error is achieved for 5 and 20 us chirps, respectively. The locations of nonlinearity-induced spurs in the IF spectrum are predicted via analysis of the DFT of FM error in generated chirps, with higher frequency DAC updates weakening these spurs and moving them out of the IF band of interest. Simulation in a coherent, monostatic radar model reveals that deterministic phase error from the chirp nonlinearity arises at a level above -70 dBc/Hz at 1 MHz offset, dominating random phase noise and setting the IF SNDR for R < 23 m and R = 92 m around 40 dB and 24 dB, respectively.

</details>


### [475] [A Digital Twin Platform Enabling Monolithic Crystal-Free Bluetooth Low Energy Single-Chip Sensor Motes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24277)
*Brandon P. Hippe,Jacob N. Louie,Dingyu Zhou,Alfonso Cortés,Filip Maksimovic,Tengfei Chang,David C. Burnett*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper presents a digital twin ecosystem for Bluetooth Low-Energy (BLE) systems-on-chips (SoCs) aimed at improving pre-silicon validation for novel wireless device development, showcasing effective communication capabilities with commercial BLE devices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of pre-silicon validation in novel wireless architectures due to a lack of development platforms and the high cost associated with failed tapeouts.

Method: Development of a digital twin ecosystem for BLE with physical-layer control, utilizing various RF front ends and digital baseband implementations, including integration with SDR and FPGA.

Result: Demonstrated communication of sensor data with BLE devices, achieving receiver sensitivities of -82 dBm, which exceeds BLE specification requirements.

Conclusion: The proposed digital twin ecosystem can provide cost-effective validation tools for future wireless devices and is adaptable to other communication protocols.

Abstract: Low-power wireless-capable systems-on-chips (SoCs) are critical for researching many of our current environmental issues. The scale at which these devices are needed for many applications necessitates innovation in their design to reduce the various capital and labor costs involved with operating an extensive sensor network. This can be difficult for devices with novel wireless architectures, as many emerging architectures lack commercially available development platforms. This makes pre-silicon validation challenging, and the impact of a failed tapeout is unacceptable when the cost is of primary concern for these devices. In this work, we propose a digital twin ecosystem for Bluetooth Low-Energy (BLE) with physical-layer (PHY) control intended for novel device development and demonstrated through use with crystal-free single-chip sensor motes. We present this system operating with multiple RF front ends and digital baseband implementations, including a commercially available Software Defined Radio (SDR) with synthesized RTL and embedded firmware, along with an existing crystal-free SoC front end and FPGA digital baseband. These configurations are shown to be capable of communicating sensor data with commercially available BLE devices and achieving receiver sensitivities up to -82 dBm, exceeding the minimum BLE specification. This approach is extendable to other hardware and communication protocols and promises to enable inexpensive, reusable validation and verification tools for novel wireless devices.

</details>


### [476] [Posterior-Aware Differential Channel Tracking for Reliable Single-Stream DAB+ Passive Radar](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24385)
*Kai Wu,Brendan Hall,Zhongqin Wang,Andrew Zhang,Jay Guo*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents a method for enhancing channel state information (CSI) estimation in DAB+ passive radar, achieving significant error reduction and improved target sensing through a novel identification and tracking strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To leverage DAB+ signals for passive radar while addressing challenges in CSI estimation due to differential encoding of communication symbols.

Method: The study formulates the issue as a differential CSI tracking problem, using previously tracked CSI for prediction and applying a maximum a posteriori approach along with linear minimum mean squared error fusion to enhance estimation stability.

Result: The proposed method reduces CSI estimation error by over 15 dB and improves median target-to-background ratio by more than 11 dB under random fading conditions, validated through simulations and experiments in Sydney.

Conclusion: The approach guarantees improved performance in symbol and CSI estimation, validated by enhanced range-Doppler maps for commercial aircraft detection.

Abstract: Digital audio broadcasting plus (DAB+) is an attractive illuminator for passive radar because it provides persistent, high-power, and geographically widespread very high frequency (VHF) orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) signals.
